rulings.Case Title: RALPH OLIVERIO VS. TRANSDEV SERVICES, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings And Memorandum Of Points And Authorities
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, August 6, 2018, Line 2, DEFENDANT TRANSDEV SERVICES, INC.'s Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings And Memorandum Of Points And Authorities. Defendant Transdev Services, Inc.'s motion for judgment on the pleadings on the second cause of action for violation of Business & Professions Code 17200 is granted with twenty days leave to amend. Plaintiff Ralph Oliverio's request to hold this motion in abeyance until he files a motion for reconsideration is denied. This motion is ripe for resolution now based on the complaint filed by Mr. Oliverio and there is no persuasive explanation why an unfiled motion will have any effect on the sole issue on this motion whether the second cause of action states a valid UCL claim based on the unlawful prong. Because they are referenced and made mandatory in SF Transportation Code 1106(j), SFMTA minimum insurance requirements constitute a law for purposes of the unlawful prong of the UCL. However, Mr. Oliverio's UCL claim fails because he has not identified any law, including the SFMTA minimum insurance requirements, that imposes an obligation on Transdev that Transdev allegedly violated. To the extent Mr. Oliverio contends that Transdev is liable on conspiracy or aiding and abetting theories, Mr. Oliverio does not allege facts showing that Transdev owed a duty to him or what Transdev did that constitutes either conspiring or aiding and abetting Yellow Cab in violating the minimum insurance requirements. (Applied Equip. Corp. v. Litton Saudi Arabia Ltd. (1994) 7 Cal.4th 503, 511(tort liability arising from conspiracy presupposes that the coconspirator owes a duty to the plaintiff recognized by law)). If he can do so in good faith, Mr. Oliverio has leave to remedy these defects and also to allege a negligence cause of action against Transdev. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Transdev is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/HK)

rulings.Case Title: CARISSA COPELAND VS. MOGOS BERHE
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion To Consolidate Claims
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, August 6, 2018, Line 3, PLAINTIFF CARISSA COPELAND's Motion To Consolidate Claims. Plaintiff Carissa Copeland's motion to consolidate claims is granted and the trial date for all three consolidated claims is now May 13, 2019. This case is now consolidated with the two UIM cases (Copeland v. Allstate Northbrook Insurance Company #0352754659TGD and Copeland v. GEICO #0495428040101011) for all purposes. Ms. Copeland may file an amended complaint alleging all three claims in this case. Ms. Copeland has shown that there is likely to be substantial overlap of the evidence in this case and the two UIM cases warranting the consolidation of all three. The interests of judicial economy and avoidance of possible inconsistent results outweighs the insurers' interests in having the UIM claims adjudicated by arbitration and the interests of the defendants in this case in having the claim against them tried separately from the UIM claims. Per the stipulation of the parties to this case, the trial date is continued to May 13, 2019 and all discovery deadlines are triggered off the new trial date. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. Counsel for Ms. Copeland is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/HK)

rulings.Case Title: KARA L. ESBORG VS. COMCAST CORP. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT And/Or Summary Adjudication
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, August 6, 2018, Line 13, DEFENDANT COMCAST OF CALIFORNIA III, INC. MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT And/Or Summary Adjudication. (part 1 of 2 of tentative ruling) Defendant Comcast of California III, Inc.s motion for summary judgment is denied and its alternative motion for summary adjudication is denied as to the first and second causes of action for general negligence and premises liability and granted as to the third cause of action for products liability. Plaintiff Kara Esborg's complaint frames the issues which need to be addressed in a summary motion. Ms. Esborg's complaint alleges: 1) that she collided with one or more low hanging wires attributable to Comcast; 2) Comcast negligently constructed, installed, monitored and maintained those wires; and 3) Comcast negligently constructed, managed and maintained the property where those wires were attached. Comcast has not satisfied its summary motion burden as to the negligence and premises liability claims. As to the first issue raised by Comcast that Ms. Esborg did not collide with a wire attributable to it, none of the facts in Comcast's separate statement, considered singly or collectively, negates its connection to the wire(s) Ms. Esborg collided with. The closest fact to this point is Fact 29 that "Comcast does not manufacture, design or sell any of the cables or clamps involved." However, not manufacturing, designing or selling a wire or clamp does not mean that Comcast does not buy or use wires and clamps. Moreover, Ms. Esborg's foundation objection to the portion of Mr. Leto's declaration cited in support of Fact 29 is sustained. As to the second issue raised by Comcast that it had no notice of any low hanging wire on the block where the incident occurred, even if this is true which it appears to be, it does not address all of Ms. Esborg's allegations why Comcast is liable for negligence and premises liability and thus does not satisfy Comcast's summary motion burden of showing that it was not negligent. Comcast's Facts 6 and 7 that its utility cable near the location of the incident was required to be installed per its procedures and in compliance with industry standards does not establish that the cable was in fact installed per proper procedures or industry standards. Nor does Fact 9 stating that clamps have a normal life of 50 or more years establish that the clamps used to attach Comcast wires that Ms. Esborg may have collided with were properly functioning at the time of the incident. Comcast's separate statement does not proffer any facts refuting Ms. Esborg's allegations that Comcast is liable for negligent construction and installation of the wire(s) she collided or negligent construction of the property where those wires were attached. As to the third issue raised by Comcast of superseding cause based on the hypothesis of a "likelihood that an oversized commercial truck pulled down the cable" (Comcast Memo 9:17), Comcast's separate statement contains no facts about a truck of any kind or anything else that could conceivably be a superseding cause. As the fourth issue raised by Comcast that it did not breach a duty of care to Ms. Esborg, that issue is merely a repeat of the first three issues and is not supported by any additional facts. Ms. Esborg does not oppose summary adjudication in favor of Comcast on the products liability claim. (end of part 1 of 2 of tentative ruling)=(302/HK)

rulings.Case Title: KARA L. ESBORG VS. COMCAST CORP. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT , Or In The Alternative Summary Adjudication
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, August 6, 2018, Line 13, DEFENDANT PACIFIC BELL TELEPHONE COMPANY's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT , Or In The Alternative Summary Adjudication. Defendant Pacific Bell Telephone Company's motion for summary judgment is denied and its alternative motion for summary adjudication is denied as to the first and second causes of action for general negligence and premises liability and granted as to the third cause of action for products liability. Plaintiff Kara Esborg's complaint frames the issues which need to be addressed in a summary motion. Ms. Esborg's complaint alleges: 1) that she collided with one or more low hanging wires attributable to Pacific Bell; 2) Pacific Bell negligently constructed, installed, monitored and maintained those wires; and 3) Pacific Bell negligently constructed, managed and maintained the property where those wires were attached. Pacific Bell has not satisfied its summary motion burden as to the negligence and premises liability claims. As to the first issue raised by Pacific Bell that Ms. Esborg did not collide with a wire attributable to it, the only fact proffered by Pacific Bell that negates its connection to the wire(s) which Ms. Esborg collided with is its Fact 10 that "A firefighter¿said it was a Comcast cable." Ms. Esborg's hearsay objections to the portion of the first Twomey declaration and her deposition which is cited in support of Fact 10 are sustained, leaving no admissible evidence to establish Fact 10. Without any admissible evidence to support Fact 10, Pacific Bell has not satisfied its summary motion burden showing that the wire(s) Ms. Esborg collided with are not attributable to it. As to the second issue raised by Pacific Bell that it had no actual notice of any low hanging wire on the block where the incident occurred, even if this is true which it appears to be, it does not address all of Ms. Esborg's allegations why Pacific Bell is liable for negligence and premises liability and thus does not satisfy Pacific Bell's summary motion burden of showing that it was not negligent. Pacific Bell's separate statement does not proffer any facts refuting Ms. Esborg's allegations that Pacific Bell is liable for negligent construction and installation of the wire(s) she collided or negligent construction, management and maintenance of the property where those wires were attached. Ms. Esborg's objections to the new evidenced submitted with Pacific Bell's reply papers are sustained since, absent an extraordinary situation, a party against whom a summary motion is filed is entitled to at least 61 days' notice of the evidence supporting the motion before having to file opposition papers to the motion. Ms. Esborg does not oppose summary adjudication in favor of Pacific Bell on the products liability claim. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Ms. Esborg is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/HK)

rulings.Case Title: MARTIFER-SILVERADO FUND I, LLC VS. TALESUN SOLAR USA LTD
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion To Quash Martifer Silverado Fund I, Llcs Deposition Subpoena For Production Of Business Records To Reed Smith, Llp
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, August 6, 2018, Line 1, DEFENDANT TALESUN SOLAR USA LTD'S Motion To Quash Martifer Silverado Fund I, Llcs Deposition Subpoena For Production Of Business Records To Reed Smith, Llp. Pro Tem Judge James Fleming, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Plaintiff served a subpoena seeking litigation-hold correspondence and related documents from defendant's former attorneys. Defendant moved to quash, citing attorney-client privilege. Plaintiff opposed the motion, arguing that defendant has failed to produce relevant emails. Plaintiff contends that the emails were in existence when defendant became aware of the possibility of litigation and should have been preserved. Plaintiff argues that federal decisions (which California often follows) permit discovery of litigation hold correspondence in cases of claimed spoliation of evidence. Plaintiff fails to cite any California authority adopting the federal doctrine of privilege. Further, while California excepts from attorney-client privilege communications to commit or plan a crime or fraud (Evidence Code Section 956), this discovery seeks the opposite: correspondence to prevent a crime or fraud. Given the absence of any California authority supporting a waiver of the attorney-client privilege here, the motion to quash will be granted. Because the case presents a good faith dispute over a novel issue of law, sanctions will be denied. Counsel to moving party shall submit a proposed order in Word format consistent with the foregoing by email to jameshfleming@gmail.com at least 24 hours prior to the scheduled hearing. If this Tentative Ruling is uncontested and said proposed order is timely submitted, no appearance by moving party is required. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to jamesfleming@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. Unless the matter is uncontested and the proposed order is submitted by email as stated above, the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: MARTIFER-SILVERADO FUND I, LLC VS. TALESUN SOLAR USA LTD
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Compliance With Defendant'S Verified Responses To Request For Production Nos. 1-9 And 21
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, August 6, 2018, Line 1, PLAINTIFF MARTIFER-SILVERADO FUND I, LLC'S Motion To Compel Compliance With Defendant'S Verified Responses To Request For Production Nos. 1-9 And 21. (Part 1 of 2) Pro Tem Judge James Fleming, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Plaintiff moves to compel compliance with defendant's previous responses to requests for production nos. 1-9 and 21 which, plaintiff contends, promised production of emails which were not subsequently produced. Plaintiff has further produced some evidence that relevant emails were in existence when defendant became aware of the likelihood of litigation and should have been preserved. Subsequent discovery indicates that the emails may have been lost or destroyed due to replacement of the relevant servers by defendant's parent company. The Court finds that defendant's promise to produce documents was expressly conditional ("if available and in the possession, custody and control of Defendant"). Further, it has not been demonstrated that the emails were "available and in the possession, custody and control of Defendant" at the time of the document production. Further, if, as defendant contends, plaintiff is really seeking further responses to the requests, plaintiff's time to seek that remedy has run. See Code of Civil Procedure Section 2031.310. Discovery sanctions are available for intentional spoliation under Code of Civil Procedure Section 2023. Cedars-Sinai Med. Ctr. v. Superior Court, 18 Cal.4th 1 (1988). But plaintiff has failed to distinguish between intentional and negligent spoliation as required and has failed to give notice of any request for sanctions, as required. Code of Civil Procedure Section 2023.040. (End of Part 1 of 2) =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: JOSE RODRIGUEZ VS. THOMAS H. TASHJIAN ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, August 6, 2018, Line 5, DEFENDANT FOLEY CONSTRUCTION's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT. Defendant Foley Construction's motion for summary judgment is denied. Foley fails to satisfy its initial summary judgment burden to show that its negligent exercise of retained control of the premises did not affirmatively contribute to Mr. Rodriquez's claimed injuries and, even if it did, there is a triable dispute whether its negligent exercise of retained control affirmatively contributed to Mr. Rodriquez's claimed injuries. (Hooker v. Department of Transportation (2002) 27 Cal.4th 198, 212, fn.3, and 214.) Fairly read, the complaint seeks to impose liability based on the exception to the Privette doctrine articulated in Hooker. The evidence on this motion also demonstrates that Foley constructed the elevator shaft and failed to ensure that safety measures were followed to prevent persons from falling down the shaft. (Foley Depo. 43:13-14; 58:21-59:25; 61:9-62:5; 84:10-13; 86:19-24; Rodriguez Dec. par. 5.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Mr. Rodriguez is required to prepare a proposed order that repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/HK)

rulings.Case Title: TIMOTHY A BONNICI VS. CHARLES MCMACKIN ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion For Relief From Judgment [Ccp Sec 473(B)]
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, August 6, 2018, Line 12, PLAINTIFF TIMOTHY BONNICI's Motion For Relief From Judgment [Ccp Sec 473(B)]. Plaintiff Timothy Bonnici's motion for relief from judgment is denied. Mr. Bonnici is not entitled to CCP 473(b) discretionary relief from the judgment filed January 24, 2018 because he did not make this motion within a reasonable time and he has not shown that the judgment was entered against him due to mistake, inadvertence, surprise or excusable neglect. Mr. Bonnici is not entitled to CCP 473(b) mandatory relief from the judgment based on his attorney's declaration of fault because the declaration of Mr. Molinari fails to attest to his or his office's fault that led to the entry of the judgment. Nowhere in the declaration is there any unambiguous statement regarding what mistake, inadvertence, surprise or neglect led to entry of the judgment or who made it. The conclusory sentence that "Any problems with the operation of my office, including, but not limited to calendaring of hearings and response times, fall to me, and I accept this responsibility" is not a substitute for the identification of the act(s) and/or inaction(s) that led to entry of the judgment and who committed those act(s) and/or inaction(s). This is particularly the case where, as here, the declaration seems to suggest, when read in conjunction with the opposition papers, that Mr. Molinari was allowing the unauthorized practice of law by Mr. Carter to occur. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/HK)

rulings.Case Title: TITILAYO ALA VS. CH2M HILL COMPANIES LTD. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Plaintiff To Provide Further Responses To Requests For Production, Set One
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Monday, August 6, 2018, Line 2, DEFENDANT CH2M HILL COMPANIES LTD. Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Plaintiff To Provide Further Responses To Requests For Production, Set One (Part 1 of 2) Pro Tem Judge James Fleming, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Defendants seeks to compel plaintiff, a current employee of defendants, to make further responses to certain requests for production calling for plaintiff to identify documents (apparently largely consisting of electronically stored information or "ESI" residing on defendants' own systems) supporting her contentions in this case. Defendants contend that plaintiff may have the assistance of her counsel (or e-counsel or consultants) to identify and evaluate documents and ESI for response. Defendants assert that plaintiff has access to a wide range of ESI stored on her work computer but defendants nevertheless oppose any "audit" of possibly relevant communications on grounds of privacy and confidentiality. Plaintiff objects that defendants' requested scheme for identifying such ESI is unduly burdensome, that further responses may reveal attorney work product, that the ESI is equally available to defendants, and that plaintiff needs the assistance of counsel to adequately determine what materials support plaintiff's contentions. Otherwise, plaintiff has agreed to produce responsive documents. Defendants' motion shall be granted to the following extent and otherwise denied: 1. Plaintiff shall make further responses to any of defendants' requests for production which expressly rely upon plaintiff's contentions. Said further responses shall be as to plaintiff's personally-maintained paper files and as to ESI residing on plaintiff's office computer and/or on any of plaintiff's personal electronic devices and shall be made within 30 days of the date hereof; plaintiff shall not at this time be required to respond as to documents not personally maintained by plaintiff or to ESI stored on defendants' systems other than plaintiff's office computer and personal devices; 2. Plaintiff shall be permitted to make a complete copy of any personally-maintained paper files and any ESI stored on plaintiff's office computer for purposes of consultation with plaintiff's counsel or consultants; defendants may move for a protective order as to any private or confidential information contained therein; (End of part 1 of 2) =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: GAWFCO ENTERPRISES, INC. VS. UBER TECHNOLOGIES, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, August 6, 2018, Line 9, DEFENDANT UBER TECHNOLOGIES, INC.'s DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT. Defendant Uber Technologies Inc.'s demurrer to all four causes of action in plaintiff Gawfco Enterprises, Inc.'s first amended complaint is overruled as to the first, second, and third causes of action for breach of contract, common counts, and fraud and is sustained with twenty days leave to amend as to the fourth cause of action for violation of Bus. & Prof. Code 17200. Liberally construed, the FAC sufficiently alleges breach of an oral contract and common counts by pleading the legal effect of the relevant terms of the oral agreement and Uber's conduct breaching that agreement: Uber's promise to pay with debit cards and replace them if they expired in exchange for the right to place promotional materials at Gawfso's gas stations and Uber's failure to replace the expired cards. Gawfco has also alleged with the requisite particularity its fraud cause of action by alleging what misrepresentations were made, by whom, when, and how. The failure to include the full names of Uber employees making the representations and to allege their authority to speak for Uber is not fatal to the claim. (Miles v. Deutsche Bank National Trust Co. (2015) 236 Cal.App.4th 394, 403 (Reversing trial court order sustaining demurrer to a fraud claim where "The primary omission in the allegations is that plaintiff does not identify the names of the people he spoke to nor their authority to speak. In our view, this omission falls comfortably in the realm of information that lies more in the possession of defendants.")). Gawfco's request to amend its section 17200 claims comports with California's policy of liberally allowing amendments to pleadings and thus is granted. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Gawfco is required to prepare a proposed order that repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/HK)

rulings.Case Title: REBECCA CAMPING VS. CHIPOLTLE SERVICES, LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For Reconsideration Of Court'S Order Denying Defendant Chipotle Services, Llc'S Petition To Compel Arbitration
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, August 6, 2018, Line 10, DEFENDANT CHIPOTLE SERVICES, LLC's Motion For Reconsideration Of Court'S Order Denying Defendant Chipotle Services, Llc'S Petition To Compel Arbitration. Defendant Chipotle Services, LLC's motion for reconsideration of the June 6, 2018 order denying its petition to compel arbitration is granted and, after reconsideration, plaintiff Rebecca Camping is required to arbitrate all of her claims except her PAGA claim and this case, including the non-arbitrable PAGA claim, is stayed pending the completion of arbitration proceedings. The declaration of Hadley Brink, which relies on statements made by Ms. Camping at the May 14, 2018 hearing, provides new facts to warrant reconsideration of the earlier order. In light of this new evidence, Chipotle has satisfied its burden of showing that Ms. Camping electronically signed the arbitration agreement. While the arbitration agreement is procedurally unconscionable, Ms. Camping has not shown that the agreement is substantively unconscionable. The failure to attach the JAMS rules is a minor issue. The agreement does not have an unconscionable fee shifting provision. Paragraph 7 of the agreement provides that Ms. Camping is not required to pay any cost or expense that she would not be required to pay if her claims were adjudicated in court. Paragraph 8 refers to the arbitrator's discretion to award fees, but that discretion is "subject to applicable law." The carve-out for injunctive relief is not unconscionable. Paragraph 8, which provides that the parties can go to court for provisional remedies, repeats the right to provisional remedies that is in CCP 1281.8. The arbitration obligations of the agreement are bilateral. There is no carve-out for employer type claims to be adjudicated in court. (See Fitz v. NCR Corp. (2004) 118 Cal.App.4th 702, 725.) The agreement does not limit Ms. Camping's ability to conduct discovery. Ms. Camping mischaracterizes the JAMS rule, which provides that each party may take at least one deposition. The court severs and stays prosecution of Ms. Camping's PAGA claim. (Iskanian v. CLS Transp. Los Angeles, LLC (2014) 59 Cal.4th 348, 384.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for Chipotle is required to prepare a proposed order that repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/HK)

rulings.Case Title: TRIMARK ERF, INC. VS. TERRAVANT WINE COMPANY, LLC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: APPLICATION AND HEARING FOR RIGHT TO ATTACH ORDER
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Monday, August 6, 2018, Line 11, PLAINTIFF TRIMARK ERF, INC.'s APPLICATION AND HEARING FOR RIGHT TO ATTACH ORDER. Plaintiff Trimark ERF, Inc.'s application for a right to attach order and order for a writ of attachment is granted. No opposition filed. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order on the Judicial Council form which states that the motion was not opposed and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/HK)
